data.title: Eldorado Gold: Rising Costs And Lower Production Paint A Grim Outlook This Year
data.description:
Canadian miner Eldorado Gold (EGO) reported quarterly results last week with better-than-expected revenue amid a gold bull run that took the metal's price above $3,000 an ounce for the first time. However, net income came in slightly below estimates due to higher operating costs at its major mining projects in Turkey and Greece. Despite this, EGO's share price has performed well so far this year, trading up ~30%, driven by expectations that the gold price will continue to rise fueled by an uncertain macroeconomic environment and rising global trade tensions.

In my previous article on EGO, I mentioned that the share price was at an inflection point, around $15, following a correction that began in October last year and continued through mid-February. However, I highlighted that excellent revenue results, coupled with sound cost management, had allowed the company to expand its profit margins and accumulate record operating cash flow during the last quarter of 2024, positioning it well for the start of this year.

With the first-quarter numbers in view and progress on the Skouries project, I maintain a predominantly optimistic outlook on the company's future, despite a slight increase in projected costs due to the impact of tariffs. However, and in addition to the latter, while my opinion on EGO stock remains bullish in the medium to long term, driven by a gold price that still has significant upside potential, EGO stock appears to be fairly valued at current prices as the company faces a year of potential lower production and increased costs, which leads me to doubt the sustainability of the price increase over the coming quarters.

Eldorado's Q1 2025 Results

Operating Results

Eldorado’s gold production reached 115,893 ounces during the first quarter of 2025, in line with the company’s expectations, although slightly down 1% year-over-year (Y/Y) and 26% below the ~155,000 ounces mined during the final quarter of last year. Management, which expects production to ramp up in the second half of the year, decided to keep its guidance for the rest of the year unchanged at between 460,000 and 500,000 ounces of gold, which represents 8% below 2024 production from the midpoint of the range.

The Lamaque project (Quebec, Canada) achieved gold production of 40,438 ounces during the first quarter of the year, a contraction of more than 4% compared to the same period last year, impacted by lower grades and recoveries that were partially offset by higher average throughput at the processing plant. Meanwhile, the company’s two major projects in Turkey, Kisladag and Efemçukuru, totaled 44,319 ounces (+18% Y/Y) and 19,307 ounces (+4% Y/Y), respectively, both registering strong performances with improved stockpile throughput in 2024 and higher average gold grades, in line with the plan for the quarter. The exception was the Olympias mine (Greece), which achieved a lower-than-expected production of 11,829 ounces (-37% Y/Y), impacted by plant operational issues related to a pyrite leak. Unfortunately, this resulted in 25% lower gold sales for the project, which was offset by an increase in the realized gold price to end with virtually flat revenues compared to the first quarter of 2024. As a result, the company also revealed an increase in cash costs, impacted by lower gold sales and higher royalties paid.

In terms of operating costs, the company’s total all-in sustaining costs [AISC] amounted to $1,559 per ounce sold, up 23% year-over-year, while cash costs reached $1,153 per ounce (+25% Y/Y), driven by higher royalty expenses in Greece and Turkey following record gold prices, coupled with higher labor and income tax costs. Due to these, management now forecasts an increase of around 10% to ~$1,420 per ounce in 2025 and up from $1,285 in 2024 after having managed to keep it relatively stable throughout last year, with an increase of 5%. Meanwhile, cash costs are expected to be around $1,030 per ounce, down from $1,153 in the first quarter and compared to an average of $940 per ounce sold in 2024. This does not yet consider the impact of US tariffs, particularly on its Canadian business unit, which are expected to add up to an additional $4 per ounce to cash costs and $6 per ounce to ASIC for this year. Despite this, the company expects to meet its annual cost guidance.

Regarding AISC margins, the increase in operating costs adversely impacted them, which contracted compared to the previous record quarter, although not dramatically. EGO's operating margin also remained at high levels compared to previous quarters, although the possibility of further deterioration remains open in the coming quarters as long as the gold price does not maintain current growth levels.

Beyond the company's operational efficiency, which allowed it to expand its margins last year, I think that consecutive cost increases this year, due to labor cost inflation and the impact of tariffs, will likely continue to be a drag on the company in the coming quarters. If not accompanied by an increase in gold prices of equal or greater magnitude, these increases could significantly erode the company's margins in the short term. On the other hand, my outlook for the gold price remains bullish, although I think we will see a more gradual rise over the next year, with possible profit-taking and fluctuations as new developments emerge in the trade war between the United States and China. At the same time, while increases in the price of its main product are significant for the company, mining companies are lagging behind in this product, requiring them to spend enormous amounts of money to sustain their level of activity.

Progress in Skouries

Eldorado presented progress on its Skouries project in Greece with a corporate video showcasing the latest developments. The company reported 66% completion of Phase 2, making significant progress in workforce development, which had presented some issues in 2024 due to the shortage of skilled workers in the country. Capital investment remained in line with expectations, and project capex is expected to be ~$425 million in 2025, up from $325 million in 2024. The company expects to begin first gold production in Q1 2026 and commercial production by mid-2026, according to management's timeline. The company maintained its guidance for copper production of 45-60 million pounds and gold production of 135,000-155,000 ounces starting next year for the Skouries project, which would increase the company's total production by approximately 30% compared to this year's estimate.

Financial Results

Revenue in the first quarter of 2025 increased 38% Y/Y to $355M, up from $255M in the same period last year and exceeding the consensus expectation by nearly $24M. This increase was primarily due to a significantly higher average realized gold price of $2,933 per ounce, compared to $2,086 per ounce in the first quarter of 2024, while the quantity of gold sold remained virtually unchanged year-over-year at 116,263 ounces, marking a sharp decrease from 156,800 ounces in the prior quarter.

The company also generated an adjusted net income of ~$56 million, or $0.28 per share, slightly higher than last year's earnings despite the impact of higher production costs, although below previous estimates. The net result was affected by a realized loss of ~$11 million from derivative financial instruments used by the company (gold collars) to hedge against potential declines in gold prices. Excluding these extraordinary items, EGO operations generated a positive net balance of $72 million, or $0.35 per share, although the company also revealed that it maintains an additional $63 million in unrealized losses from these same hedging instruments.

On the other hand, operating cash flow reached $138 million in the first quarter of 2025, comfortably exceeding the $95 million from the previous year, with negative free cash flow [FCF] of $22 million. However, excluding capital expenditures on the Skouries project, FCF remained positive at $76M, compared to $34M in Q1 2024, reflecting the company continuing to effectively capitalize on the strong momentum in both gold prices and the rally in copper, which reached a new all-time high in March above $5 per pound.

Finally, the company maintains a strong balance sheet, with total liquidity of $1.2B at quarter end, distributed between $978M in cash and $241M available through credit facilities, compared to $1.1B in the previous quarter, which combined with a good operating cash flow generation provides enough financial flexibility to continue to smoothly meet the company's high capital costs in Skouries.

Valuation & Technicals

After breaking through the bearish resistance that began in late October, EGO shares surpassed $20, reaching 2016 highs before correcting to the $18 level amid talks about a possible US-China trade deal, briefly boosting the rest of the market and punishing gold and mining companies. This drop may also be due to the fact that many investors took advantage of a 35% rise in the first few months of the year to take profits and wait for better entry prices.

While I continue to think that the medium- to long-term outlook for gold remains very positive, with many global central banks entering a phase of quantitative easing and others maintaining strong demand for the golden metal, EGO is likely to regain its bullish trend at some point this year. However, in the short term, it could be impacted by lower mine production and rising costs as the gold appreciation could fail to offset, again, a lower throughput as it did in Q1.

In my previous article on EGO, I highlighted the fact that the company has lagged its peers. However, after being one of the best performers over a 1- to 3-month period, EGO shares remains attractively priced relative to the industry average and its closest competitors, although the gap has narrowed.

In terms of valuation, the company is also one of the most undervalued in terms of price to operating cash flow, after posting an excellent year in 2024, and it also lags a broader list of industry peers in terms of EV/EBITDA, including the major producers.

Considering the trailing 12-month [TTM] operating cash flow per diluted share as of March 31 of 3.33 and multiplying it by a multiple of 5.856, we get a share price estimate of ~$19.50, signaling a possible fair value at these prices but assuming no CFO growth over the next year, which is quite conservative. On the other hand, with an EBITDA per share of ~$3.54 and applying a forward EV/EBITDA of 5.108 we get an enterprise value per share of 18.07, equivalent to ~$3.73B of total EV, which is slightly below the current EV of $4B.

Risks

One of EGO's main issues that I see is its growing percentage of revenues coming from low-profile jurisdictions, such as Greece and Turkey (65% TTM as of Q1 2025), which will increase even further starting in 2026 when the Skouries plant comes online. This is likely a factor in investors' valuation of the company, who tend to favor mining companies operating in more stable jurisdictions with a more attractive profile. Particularly in Turkey, labor costs have experienced a sharp increase, primarily driven by increasing minimum wages, to offset a rising inflation in the country. As a result of this, operating costs in Turkey increased by 37% last year, despite a decrease in the last quarter, well above those recorded in other jurisdictions where the company also operates, such as Greece (+15% Y/Y) and Canada (+1.5% Y/Y).

On the other hand, delays in the Skouries operations schedule could also weigh on stock prices in the coming quarters, as could a reduction in the project's production guidance. While I think the market has not yet fully priced in the potential of this project, higher expectations surrounding it and subpar updates could deteriorate valuations in the short term.

Conclusion

I'm lowering my rating on EGO shares to hold despite strong fundamentals and a still bullish outlook for gold. The threat of rising operating costs due to tariffs and high exposure to low-profile jurisdictions make me cautious about current valuations, which are also indicating to be at fair value. Furthermore, the company's dependence on progress at Skouries, its most ambitious project, makes it especially vulnerable to short-term price setbacks, as long as the project doesn't become operational by early 2026.

data.title: Weis Markets: Stock Price A Bit Ahead Of Itself
data.description:
Weis Markets (WMK) common stock price has gotten a bit ahead of itself.

As the last article noted, Weis Markets is known for its consistency with a general growth plan that has remained the same for basically decades. Generally, Weis will open a few stores (either through acquisition or construction) and possibly close a few less stores to result in upper single digit growth with an occasional bigger bump.

This kind of plan, when combined with a slowly rising dividend, is designed for a total return in the low teens.

However, as the price-earnings ratio shows above, the stock price valuation has gotten ahead of that long-term plan. Generally, the slow growth allows long-term holders to have a decent average return even though that return may happen "all at once" followed by a few years of stagnation of the stock price.

The stock price has clearly pulled back from its highs. But as the chart shows above, it probably could pull back more. Personally, I would consider waiting for a price-earnings ratio in the low teens. The stock has been there plenty of times in its history after brief run-ups.

This management does not do a monthly presentation nor does it do "road shows" to support the stock price (and generate more institutional interest). Instead, this family-owned business prefers to allow the results to speak for themselves.

In a notoriously brutal industry, the results are generally regarded as quite good. I happen to like everything except the current stock price. If history repeats, I expect to be rewarded with a better price in the future.

Shares Owned

This is a family-run company, as shown below:

For the most part, the holdings shown above do not change materially. What is shown above is a representative group of family members that vote together. Those family members listed control about 40% of the shares outstanding. That likely means that they have the votes to run the company as the family "sees fit" to run the company.

Family run companies, on average, usually do better than average returns over the long term. With a big block of stock, the family can resist short-term market demands that cost the long-term return of the stock price.

Institutional Holdings

In addition to the family holdings shown above, institutions likewise own a fairly large number of shares.

This means that usually only about a quarter of the available shares are part of the regular float that is a possible buy or sell. Even though the stock is listed on the NYSE. It is highly advisable for an investor to consider using limit orders.

The other consideration is that since the institutional holdings constitute so much of the available stock, an exit by a major institutional holder could take some time due to the small amount of stock available for the float.

The first slide shows a rather small daily volume considering the size of the company.

Area Of Operation

Weis Market began operations in Sunbury, Pennsylvania (where the headquarters remains).

Operations have expanded to the present number of stores as shown above. As noted before, slow expansion is likely to continue. This company tends to avoid "the big cities" while preferring less populated locations. Something like Philadelphia is likely to be avoided for a long time to come as a result.

Earnings

This company has long been noted for an above-average income margin compared to much of the industry. A very general guide would be that grocery stores make about $.005 on the sales dollar. There are some surveys that have a typical profit margin of as high as $.02 per sales dollar. Much of this tends to depend upon what you call typical and how you measure profits. The grocery store profits are made on the sales volume. It is a tremendously competitive industry in a number of ways.

Note that the net income margin is nearly 2%. That is far above a typical grocery (large chain) store margin. If the sales volumes for Weis Markets is about the same as for a typical grocery store, then these stores are extremely profitable compared to the average grocery store.

With a profit margin that tight, management has to be very careful with decisions made as it is very easy to lose a lot of money fast in the food business.

Balance Sheet

The balance sheet is a financial rock.

Note that the company has about $330 million of cash and securities on hand. The conservative management shows in the form of working capital that is likely the envy of the industry. There is also no long-term debt. Financially, this is easily one of the safest companies that I follow.

In each of the last three years, annual earnings topped $100 million. For me, that is quite an accomplishment knowing about all that cash on the balance sheet and no debt.

Summary

Weis Markets has had the same general growth strategy for decades. While the company has clearly "kept up with the times", in other ways it is very much the same Weis Markets I remember from my youth.

Grocery stores tend to thrive in good and bad times. That may be the reason the price ran up before the recent price downturn began. This is a relative safe haven during a time of uncertainty. But there is no reason to overpay for the benefits of this investment.

As I noted before, I like everything but the stock price. For me, this is a hold because I think there are an awful lot of good things that this company is doing. I would therefore not sell it. But I would wait for a better price before considering investing in the company.

Risk

Grocery stores require more management than many realize due to the very tight profit margins combined with making profits due to the volume of business done.

This makes for a brutally competitive situation as one of the easiest things in the world is to open up another store. The barriers to entry are non-existent. This store tends to avoid the worst of the competition by picking lower (sometimes urban) areas or even country locations. But for as long as I can remember, the stores are not in big cities like Philadelphia.

The Weis family has done very well running this business for decades. A loss of key personnel could prove daunting to replace in a situation like this.

data.title: Eagle Bancorp: Priced As If We Were In A Severe Recession
data.description:
Eagle Bancorp (EGBN) is a bank based in Bethesda, Maryland, and its capital gains performance has been disastrous over the past 10 years: -52.60%. After the collapse of SVB, almost all regional banks plummeted, but they recovered in the following quarters: EGBN is one of the few exceptions.

But what is so wrong with this bank? In this article, I will highlight its weaknesses and what led to its downfall. However, I can tell you in advance that there are also some positive aspects, which the market may not be taking into account.

What I don't like about this bank

It is clear that EGBN has more than one problem to have suffered such a prolonged slump of this magnitude. The first is undoubtedly its inability to adapt to the new post-pandemic macroeconomic environment. The rapid rise in the Fed Funds Rate was followed by a sharp and violent increase in the cost of deposits, thereby compromising EGBN's profitability.

The cost of deposits rose faster than loan yields improved, leading to a decline in net interest income. Today, the macroeconomic environment is changing again, and unlike last time, the goal is to lower rates once again. Futures are already pricing in several rate cuts, but EGBN's cost of deposits remains too high and in line with the Fed Funds Rate.

In Q1 2025, total interest-bearing deposits had an average cost of 3.91%, an improvement of only 15 basis points compared to the previous quarter. Furthermore, it does not help that average non-interest-bearing deposits are still declining, falling by only 2.50% compared to the previous quarter. The decline in interest rates also affected assets, with the average loan yield falling by 18 basis points compared to the previous quarter, 3 basis points more than the cost of deposits. This is probably the most complicated issue to resolve because EGBN has shown that it has no competitive advantage on deposits and is suffering from a higher for longer interest rate environment. If it has to offer an average return of almost 4% to retain depositors, it means that its customers are not very loyal. If the return on loans improved, the situation would be less complicated, but this is not the case. The net interest margin is quite low, only 2.28%.

Among other things, the investment portfolio does not help either. AFS securities yield only 2.10% on average, while HTM securities yield only 2.20%. These returns are too low for current market rates and are generating significant unrealized losses.

Adding up these losses from both AFS and HTM securities, EGBN is losing approximately $220 million, a significant amount considering that its equity in the last quarter was only $1.24 billion.

Another negative aspect that investors are not happy about is the lack of diversification in its portfolio.

67% of the loan portfolio consists of CRE loans, a category that has often underperformed during economic slowdowns/recessions. They perform very well during expansive phases of the economic cycle, but this involves taking significant risks in the opposite scenario. The data seems to be pointing toward the latter scenario, as the non-performing asset to total asset ratio is gradually increasing: in Q1 2025 it was 1.79% vs. 0.79% in Q1 2024. An increase of one percentage point may seem small, but it is not.

Finally, given that profitability is declining and non-performing assets are increasing, management has significantly cut the dividend.

In such cases, the market never reacts well, as it is a clear sign of weakness on the part of the bank.

What I like about this bank

The fundamentals of this bank are far from exciting, but in this section I would like to highlight some positive aspects that could support a bullish thesis. First, EGBN price per share has been declining for 10 years, but net interest income and TBV per share are not following the same trend.

Sure, things haven't been going well since the Fed started raising rates, but the very long-term trend is clearly upward.

The same applies to TBV per share, the main component for valuing any bank. Among other things, in this case, the investment portfolio could act as a tailwind. I showed you earlier how much the unrealized losses amounted to, but if the securities mature, these losses disappear, giving EGBN's TBV a boost. The only drag on the latter is AFS securities, which showed an unrealized loss of $116 million in Q1 2025. This figure would be added to equity if the securities were brought to maturity or if the Fed cut rates more than expected.

Finally, one last point, and perhaps the most important, is that this bank is priced as if the end of the world has come.

Its TBV is trading at just 0.45x, less than half its market capitalization. It is rather interesting to note that such negative sentiment was not even present during the peak of the great financial crisis. At the time, many banks failed, and the entire sector faced one of its worst crises, yet EGBN is now priced as if we were in a worse situation. Certainly, the recession risk has increased due to the tariffs, but even if there is a recession, I am not sure it will be as severe as in 2009.

In short, there is a strong mistrust of this bank. The market is probably convinced that non-performing assets will increase due to heavy exposure to CRE loans, or believes that there will be another dividend cut. As far as I am concerned, I believe that its fundamentals are not among the best, but perhaps the market has punished it too much. In addition, I would like to mention two aspects that lead me to believe that EGBN may be more solid than expected:

  • The first is that it has a loan-to-deposit ratio of only 80%, which indicates that its financial structure is not very rigid.
  • The second is that its CET1 ratio is 14.61%, which is very high.

While it is clear that its assets have/will have some challenges, its very cheap valuation combined with a very high CET1 ratio make it an interesting investment. After such a collapse, I find it hard to believe that it can continue to fall; its TBV per share already seems to be discounting a very negative scenario.

Clearly, if there is a recession, the downside could continue, and this is perhaps the main risk. In my opinion, EGBN could be a good investment for anyone who believes that the economy is poised to rebound and that the Fed will cut rates. If the TBV per share reaches unity, EGBN would generate triple-digit capital gains, in addition to rewarding investors with the current dividend yield of 3.60%. It is a very risky investment, so at most I would allocate a very small portion of my portfolio to it.

data.title: My Top 5 AI Stocks
data.description:
Steve here—welcome to Seeking Alpha Quant. In this article, I’ll discuss my top five stocks powering new innovations in artificial intelligence. Each one has demonstrated remarkable resilience in the face of volatile markets and has solid fundamentals to back up its Strong Buy rating. Let’s dive in.

How Artificial Intelligence Can Help Reboot Your Portfolio

They say April showers bring May flowers. But storm clouds continue to loom over Wall Street as tariffs and trade wars dominate the headlines. For investors, it’s critical to look beyond short-term market volatility and focus on the fundamentals. As artificial intelligence becomes increasingly integral to the global economy, companies embracing the megatrend in new and innovative ways will be enviable once the turmoil subsides.

As I discussed in my latest webinar on AI, heightened geopolitical tensions have led to a period of highly emotional investing. Many investors lose sight of the long-term fundamentals, instead reacting from a place of fear. But panic-selling is never the answer. Tech stocks often lose value during market instability but tend to bounce back as economic confidence improves and market conditions stabilize. We’ve already seen that principle in action, as some of my ‘Biggest Losers’ (Strong Buy stocks experiencing major declines) have already begun to gain momentum amid strong financial performance.

Many of the AI darling stocks of 2023 and 2024 have retreated sharply, but it's not because their fundamentals have changed. Remember, AI is still in its infancy, and explosive demand for hyperscale data centers, semiconductors, and other key components of the AI supply chain will likely outlast the current economic downturn.

Usage of top AI platforms is up 4x year-over-year, according to an analysis by Blackstone, while OpenAI now has over 600 million monthly active users. Revenues have also increased dramatically, with AI model revenues for the 2025 fiscal year up 16x since 2023 and 3x Y/Y to $14 billion.

Meanwhile, the cost of operating AI models such as ChatGPT has grown 99% cheaper since March 2023. As Blackstone’s Mike Forman writes:

The potential for AI is enormous. I expect it will become embedded into everything — enterprise processes, scientific discovery, medical advances, manufacturing, and robotics. Each of these use cases will require significant increases in computing power compared to today.

But as AI becomes an increasingly ubiquitous part of our lives and the economy, how can you tell which companies make for truly good investments? Seeking Alpha’s Quant System can help you navigate the frothiness of the AI market and zero in on stocks with top-tier fundamentals, powerful momentum, and industry-leading valuations.

How I Picked the Top AI Stocks

Our data-driven Quant system leverages powerful computer processing and our proprietary algorithm to analyze thousands of stocks across a diverse range of sectors. It ranks them based on hundreds of metrics across value, growth, profitability, EPS revisions, and momentum, lending unique insights about their performance and long-term outlook only available to SA Premium and PRO subscribers.

To identify the top-performing stocks in the AI universe, I’ve used the Quant system to analyze securities from three of the largest AI ETFs—the Global X Robotics & Artificial Intelligence ETF (BOTZ), the Robo Global® Robotics and Automation Index ETF (ROBO, and the Global X Artificial Intelligence & Technology ETF—(AIQ) and selected five top-performing stocks to represent a diverse range of AI opportunities.

They include tech companies that sell AI-powered solutions and non-tech firms that employ AI to boost productivity, improve products, and streamline operations. My top five AI-leveraging stocks for May have returned an average of ~32% over the past month and have an average forward price-to-earnings-growth (PEG) ratio of 0.45.

Top 5 AI Stocks for May, by Quant Rating (as of 5/9/25)

1. CommScope Holding Company, Inc. (COMM)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.1B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 5/9/25): 2 out of 537

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 5/9/25): 1 out of 41

Ranked second among all technology stocks and first among communications equipment stocks, CommScope Holding Company (COMM) offers data center infrastructure solutions for hyperscalers, among other tech infrastructure services. After taking a hit from volatile markets over the past several months, COMM has engineered a comeback by focusing on high-growth areas like fiber solutions and enterprise Wi-Fi, returning more than 30% over the past month.

The group reported strong Q1 results, including a +23% Y/Y increase in revenue to $1.11B (+23.3% Y/Y). Its enterprise fiber business grew 88% Y/Y to $213M, profiting off the explosion of data center market growth over the past year.

COMM Stock’s Quant Breakdown

Despite returning a whopping 370% over the past year, COMM still touts ‘A+’ valuation grade with a forward GAAP P/E of 4.02, an 84.7% discount from the sector median. Its forward PEG of 0.14 is an impressive 91.2% discount from the sector median.

It also demonstrates excellent growth, with Y/Y EBIT growth of 2,003.2%, a sector-crushing 18,329.4% difference from the sector median. With five upward EPS revisions and 5 upward revenue revisions over the past 90 days, analysts appear in line with SA Quant’s Strong Buy assessment.

My next pick also excels in the niche yet lucrative field of communications infrastructure, which has thrived in the age of AI.

2. Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd (CRDO)

  • Market Capitalization: $8.3B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 5/9/25): 2 out of 538

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 5/9/25): 1 out of 68

One of my top beaten-down tech stocks for 2025, Credo Technology Group (CRDO) has come back swinging. The top semiconductor stock and number-two technology stock by Quant rating, the company has carved a lucrative niche in the AI economy with its specialized high-speed connectivity products, including integrated circuits and active electrical cables (AECs).

With offices in California, Taiwan, Hong Kong, Shanghai, and Tokyo, the company’s high-speed semiconductor solutions could be exposed to escalating trade tensions between the U.S. and China, as I’ve previously discussed. However, CRDO’s turbocharged growth and strong fundamentals (all for a reasonable price) make it a resilient long-term investment.

CRDO Stock’s Quant Breakdown

CRDO trades at a solid discount, with a forward PEG of 0.66, a 57% discount from the broader technology sector. While other valuation metrics suggest some room for improvement (its F-rated forward P/E of 155.95 is a 495% premium to the sector), now is a prime time to buy before its shares make a full comeback. As SA analyst Oakoff Investments wrote this week:

CRDO stock has finally become cheap enough for GARP investors to consider after its YTD dip of >28%...Last time I assessed CRDO's valuation, the stock was trading at ~107x forward P/E ratio. At the time of this article's writing, CRDO is valued at ~76x, so the multiple contraction on the P/E level for the past 2 months amounted to 29%.

Meanwhile, Oakoff adds, “CRDO's PEG is >56% lower than the entire IT sector's median figure (0.66 vs. 1.52).” The company touts ‘A+’ growth, with forward revenue growth of 55.3% (a 717.6% difference from the sector median) and forward EBITDA growth of 145% (a 1,454% difference). Wall Street appears aligned with my thesis on CRDO, with 10 positive EPS revisions over the past 90 days and zero negatives.

CRDO Annual Estimates Revisions

Up next is another undervalued stock capitalizing on the rapid growth of data centers across the globe.

3. Seagate Technology Holdings (STX)

  • Market Capitalization: $19.9B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 5/6/25): 24 out of 538

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 5/6/25): 3 out of 27

Seagate Technology Holdings (STX) is an Ireland-based manufacturer of computer hardware such as hard disk drives (HDDs) and storage systems. The company put some analysts’ nerves to rest regarding tariffs and demand with better-than-expected earnings results at the end of last month. As SA analyst Gary Alexander wrote:

Seagate's strong Q3 earnings and minimal tariff impact have driven a 10% post-earnings stock surge, recouping all YTD losses and pushing shares up for the year. The company benefits from robust AI-driven datacenter demand, leading to significant y/y growth and larger storage module shipments

STX Stock’s Quant Breakdown

Following its most recent earnings, improvements in analyst revisions, with 15 positive EPS revisions and three negative ones, helped elevate STX from a Buy to a Strong Buy in our Quant system on May 1.

STX Quant Rating History

STX boasts a superior valuation, with an ‘A+’ forward PEG of 0.11—a 93% discount to the sector. Its forward P/E of 14.14 is also strong at a 46% discount to the sector. Forward EPS diluted growth of 87%, a 2,533% difference from the sector median, is one of many impressive metrics contributing to its ‘A+’ growth grade.

My next selection is a Quant all-star that has established itself as a critical component in the AI supply chain.

4. Celestica Inc. (CLS)

  • Market Capitalization: $10.6B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 5/9/25): 22 out of 538

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 5/9/25): 1 out of 16

A two-time Alpha Pick, Celestica Inc. (CLS) has gotten its shine back, rising 40% over the past month after getting caught up in the initial tariff sell-off in March. Currently rated the top electronic manufacturing services stock, the Toronto-based company manufactures critical electronic parts for data centers. The group has become the top player in this niche yet lucrative part of the AI supply chain, with outsize growth prospects fueled by massive hyperscaler demand.

Returning more than 96% over the past year, CLS remains a top-performing Alpha Pick. It was initially selected for the portfolio on October 16, 2023, at $25.65 per share and now trades at $95.19. The group recently raised its revenue outlook for 2025 after stellar Q1 2025 earnings. As SA analyst Bram de Haas writes on its financial position:

Management's conservative guidance has been raised, and they see opportunities to gain market share despite tariff challenges, showcasing their ability to navigate complexity…Margins are expanding, particularly in the high-growth CCS segment, driving significant earnings growth and adding value.

CLS Stock’s Quant Breakdown

CLS’s valuation has improved from a ‘C’ to a ‘B-’ over the past three months. Its forward PEG ratio of 0.53 comes at a 66% discount from the sector. That’s down from its 0.8 PEG (FWD) back when it made my list of March AI picks, reflecting an even better value proposition given its strong growth. A forward price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of 26.6 similarly reflects an excellent valuation at a 19% discount from the sector.

Growth is strong, with long-term EPS growth of 34.8, a 153% difference from the sector median. Wall Street is similarly bullish on CLS with eight positive EPS revisions and one negative EPS revision over the past 90 days.

Like CLS, this next stock has also taken up a starring role in the AI economy.

5. Freshworks Inc. (FRSH)

  • Market Capitalization: $4.2B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 5/6/25): 16 out of 538

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 5/6/25): 4 out of 187

Included within my top AI stocks for 2026, Freshworks (FRSH) remains high on my list due to its solid valuation and robust growth outlook. The cloud-based software-as-a-service company offers AI-enabled software solutions to over 68,000 customers, including American Express, Shopify, and Airbus. After beating expectations with its Q1 financial results, the India-founded company forecasted powerful AI-driven growth for the rest of the year with a revenue target of $824.3M.

As SA analyst Ahmed Abdelazim noted:

While the company's fundamentals are robust with zero debt, growing revenues, and improving free cash flow generation, I believe its stock trades at a significant discount to industry peers.

FRSH Stock’s Quant Breakdown

FRSH’s forward PEG of 0.85 comes at a 45% discount to the sector, making it a solid “growth at a reasonable price” pick given its strong growth outlook.

Profitability is also impressive, with a gross profit margin (TTM) of 84.4%, a 66.8% difference from the sector. With 16 positive EPS revisions from analysts over the past 90 days and zero negative revisions, analysts are equally bullish on FRSH’s long-term prospects.

Conclusion: When It Comes To AI, Focus On The Fundamentals

As back-and-forth tariffs continue to fuel a sweeping sell-off across Wall Street, many investors are trading from a place of fear rather than focusing on the long-term fundamentals. Rather than being one of them, consider adding resilient stocks that offer growth at a reasonable price, such as the five AI-leveraging stocks mentioned in this piece. The AI evolution is still in the early phases, and will likely remain a megatrend long after the current market turmoil subsides.

The top AI picks listed in this article are up ~32% on average over the past month, with market-crushing momentum and competitive valuations. Explore AI investing with these six Strong Buys, or consider Alpha Picks if you're seeking a limited number of Seeking Alpha Quant’s best monthly ideas from the hundreds of top quant Strong Buy-rated stocks.

data.title: Brinker International: A Good Time To EAT
data.description:
After a blockbuster year, casual dining chain Brinker International (EAT) is poised for a defining 2025. The tussle between macro headwinds pressurizing consumer discretionary spending and a continued growth led by operational efficiencies and menu optimizations is going to define the road ahead.

Traffic Recovery Against Macro Sensitivity

After facing pressures from rising costs that eroded margins and seeing lower traffic post COVID, Brinker started turning around its margin recovery efforts visibly from late 2023. A 21% traffic increase has been reported in Q3 2025 recently (for quarter ending March 2025). The turnaround strategy has been based on the executional pillars typical of casual dining chains - operational efficiencies, value positioning and menu optimizations. Clearly the strategy is working so far with traffic increasing and same store sales showing visible upticks. This also shows increasing customer engagement and stickiness (over only expansion-driven growth), primarily led by the flagship Chili's brand.

The same store sales jump has been in an environment of significant topline growth, which makes it an even stronger turnaround. The margins are also improving while the same store sales have gone up, even at the operating level. This points to greater sustainability over strategies like short-term campaigns leading to temporary upticks.

The overall traction backed by margin expansion is driven by an optimal pricing approach which has helped Brinker absorb the food cost increases due to inflation and menu mix changes. Pricing is often the holy grail in casual dining. As of now, it can be said that Brinker has been able to establish the optimal prices that are drawing in customers without impacting its financials.

Brinker has also been able to scale its revenue without a disproportionate labor cost increase, pointing to operational efficiencies. On the customer-facing side, partnership with Red Hat to drive serving robots in some places and overall unification of digital platforms to simplify customer engagement and experience, shows Brinker's commitment to tech-based efficiencies.

The story of growth at controlled expenses and enhanced customer stickiness has all the boxes ticked so far. But what about the macro headwinds? Management remains fairly confident and have revised their estimates upwards for fiscal 2025. That is great, but that covers the next quarter only. Fresh management updates in the FY 2025 Earnings Session will be key to get a guidance for the upcoming fiscal year.

I see pressures emerging on EAT due to a drop in discretionary spending. And also from the taller comparable growth ask in same stores sales after a stellar run. The current management's ability to adapt to changing environments is established given the turnaround in the past couple of years (growing stickiness at stable margins is a hug tick in an industry where moat is difficult). EAT is positioned to respond well given the cost efficiencies and customer stickiness they have built now.

However, there will be pressure on margins and topline, even if at reduced levels, before any recovery. Brinker's brand stickiness and price elasticity is expected to place them better in a lower customer spend regime than a fine dining positioning.

And How is EAT Valued

Undervalued in terms of forward EV to EBITDA of peers, but slightly above its own historical averages.

There are some concerns around the higher level of debt for EAT compared to the peers who are valued higher. However, I see some breakaway improvements in both Free Cash and EBITDA that should allay some of the debt concerns. Improving margins, as observed earlier will surely reduce the gap here.

A significant portion of the undervaluation is also because of how the peers have consistently grown and performed compared to EAT's only recent turnaround. From that perspective, the information arbitrage we have is the green shoots' strength we have seen in the turnaround so far. Markets may be waiting for sustained growth to reflect in multiple expansion, but that could be too late to invest, particularly if you believe in the early green shoots. This could actually be a good zone to time a buy.

What Should Investors Do

Summarizing the positives

  • Two quarters of same store sales increase is a confirmation of the turnaround in traffic and customer stickiness
  • Topline and margins both have been growing during this turnaround
  • Focus on cost efficiencies and pricing seems to have gone right and places EAT well for any downturn
  • Overall turnaround gives confidence in management's ability to adapt to a downturn
  • Valuation multiples seem to have not reacted to the green shoots yet. Could be an ideal time to invest before growth is factored in.
  • Free cash and margin improvements will help allay some of the debt concerns

In terms of drags

  • The debt load is higher than peers. Improving scene.
  • Sustained growth is yet to be seen. Does not impact investment thesis.
  • Valuation expensive compared to own history. But moderate overvaluation and attributable to the turnaround. In fact, could be the sweet spot to invest.
  • Macro headwinds. Although better placed than fine dine and shown customer loyalty.
  • Tougher comps for next earnings cycles after blockbuster turnaround
  • Mostly reliant on single brand Chili and not aggressively expanding. The health of the growth is more important.

Overall, it is good to note the rally in EAT is not a significant valuation expansion, but more a reflection of the turnaround in earnings. That gives an opportunity to initiate "Buy" at these levels too. Expect some stagnation or volatility if the macro headwinds are tougher, but the overall positives outweigh the negatives. It is a good time to EAT while valuations haven't reacted much to the strong turnaround signs - waiting for sustained growth and in the face of macro headwinds.
